trace.rationale: Historical MLB data shows roughly 50-55% of first innings have at least one run, with slight variation by team strength and pitcher. No specific team or pitcher info provided, so baseline estimate applies.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 52% of first innings have at least one run scored, with no specific team or pitcher information to adjust from baseline.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 48-52% chance of a scoreless first inning, but slight edge to runs due to league-wide scoring trends.
Historical data shows roughly 55% of MLB first innings have at least one run scored, with no specific team or pitcher info to adjust.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 55% of games have a run scored in the first inning, with no specific team or pitcher info to adjust.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 55-60% of first innings have at least one run scored, but no specific team or pitcher evidence is provided.
Historical base rate for runs in the first inning in MLB games is approximately 55%, based on league-wide data from recent seasons.
Historical data shows roughly 45-50% of first innings have no runs, but slight edge to runs due to typical scoring patterns.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 55-60% of first innings have at least one run scored, but without specific team/player info, a slight edge to 'Yes' is reasonable.
